mnemonics.title: RGPBAN - Graphics Pipeline Stages
mnemonics.content: RGPBAN stands for: Rasterization → Geometry Processing → Primitive Assembly → Buffer Operations → Attribute Setup → Navigation. This represents the flow of 3D graphics processing from model to screen.

mnemonics.title: SMURF - Color Model
mnemonics.content: SMURF: Subtract the Magenta, Understand the Red, Forget the Yellow - a way to remember that CMY is subtractive (inks, prints) while RGB is additive (light, screens).

mnemonics.title: GKS-PHIGS Chain
mnemonics.content: GKS (Graphical Kernel System) → Core → PHIGS (Programmer's Hierarchical Interactive Graphics System) → OpenGL. Remember: 'Great Kings Provide OpenGL' - historical evolution of graphics standards.

mnemonics.title: T. F. V. S. - 2D Transformations
mnemonics.content: T.F.V.S. = Translation, Rotation, Scaling, Shearing - the four fundamental 2D geometric transformations in computer graphics.

mnemonics.title: V. P. R. - Viewing Pipeline
mnemonics.content: V.P.R. = Viewpoint → Projection → Rasterization - the three main stages of converting 3D world coordinates to 2D screen pixels.

acronyms.usage: Old display technology using electron beams to illuminate phosphorescent pixels
Modern flat-panel display using liquid crystals to modulate light
Display technology where each pixel is an LED; used in large screens
Specialized processor for rendering graphics and images
Interface for software to interact with graphics hardware (e.g., OpenGL, DirectX)
Memory that stores depth information for visible surface determination
Measure of display refresh rate and animation smoothness
Measure of printer or scanner resolution
Additive color model for screens using primary light colors
Application of computer graphics for technical drawings
User interface using graphical elements (windows, icons, menus)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a room: X-axis goes left-right (width), Y-axis goes up-down (height), Z-axis goes in-out (depth). Your eyes are at the origin looking down the -Z axis.
Think of matrices as 'transformation machines' - each number in the matrix tells how to stretch, rotate, or shift points in space.
Raster = mosaic tiles (pixels with fixed positions), Vector = connect-the-dots (lines and curves defined by math)
Imagine transparent stacking of papers - always show the one closest to you, hiding everything behind it
Think of a photo editor's crop tool - anything outside the visible area gets cut off before rendering
Like flipbook animation - more flips per second = smoother motion. 60Hz = 60 flips per second
Think of paint pots - 1-bit = 2 colors (black/white), 8-bit = 256 colors, 24-bit = 16.7 million colors (millions of paint choices)

analogies.analogy: Like an artist canvas - each pixel position holds a color value that gets displayed. More bits per pixel = more colors available to paint with.
Like a factory assembly line - raw 3D models go in one end, get transformed, shaded, and assembled through multiple stations, finished 2D image comes out the other end.
Like having two chalkboards - you write on one while showing the other, then swap. This prevents flickering when animations update.
Like blurring sharp staircase edges (jagged pixels) to create smooth-looking slopes - just like we round corners in real life for smoother appearance.
Like wrapping gift paper around a 3D box - the flat 2D image (texture) gets stretched and adhered to the 3D surface.
Like painting with different brushes - Flat shading uses one color per face (matte), Gouraud smooths between vertices (gradient), Phong adds shiny highlights (glossy).

Like a flashlight casting shadows - 3D objects projected onto a 2D surface (screen). Perspective makes parallel lines converge (like real vision), orthographic keeps them parallel (technical drawings).
Mouse = digital finger pointing at screen; Keyboard = text input tool; Touchscreen = direct finger contact; Stylus = precision pen for detailed work.
